channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: The Sims 4 modding community is disappointed by new 'microtransaction' marketplace
item.content:encoded:
The Sims 4 has had a robust modding community for as long as it's existed. The life simulation franchise lets players build whatever life they'd like. The flexibility and freedom is a huge part of the draw. Electronic Arts has provided the framework, and the modding community expanded on it; built on top of the game, modders have added everything from more skin tones and hairstyles to nudity and sex positions. Electronic Arts has, in some ways, supported its creators, but has never officially allowed modders to charge for their creations. Modders get around that by setting up Patreon or other subscription services allowing players early access to mods, where people can support with monthly payments instead of directly for content.

The Sims 4 Marketplace and its associated The Sims Maker Program are the first official ways for players to buy custom content for the game—not mods that change the game's scripting, but new items and cosmetics. But not all The Sims 4 creators are happy. 

Related:Here's where to find Game Developer's coverage from GDC Festival of Gaming!

Announced on March 3, The Sims 4 Marketplace is disappointing to much of the community because of several key decisions—specifically, because Electronic Arts is introducing a new currency, called Moola, necessary for purchasing stuff on the marketplace and taking a 70 percent cut of every purchase. 

User-generated content is big business; UGC games and platforms like Roblox are among the most successful in the industry. Electronic Arts, it seems, wants a bite of that. The marketplace is the first time The Sims 4's console players will have access to custom content, but some of the program's problems are hard to overlook for creators.

"I personally find the Marketplace to be an ugly combination of enforced microtransactions, extraction of talent from the creators of The Sims, and perpetuation of the community’s dependence on a constant stream of content," The Sims 4 CC creator myshunosun told Game Developer. "I see this as another way for EA to monetize downloadable content. In this case, creators do the vast majority of the work while receiving only 30 percent of the profits. The custom content policy I mentioned earlier now seems ironic in this context: it suggests EA opposes creators monetizing their content unless EA directly profits from it."

Myshunosun (who uses she/they pronouns) has been making custom content, largely objects or Create-A-Sim assets like tattoos, for The Sims for 15 years. They're one of the first modders asked to develop a Creator Kit—the Cozy Kitsch Creator Kit was published and sold by Electronic Arts as an official kit in November 2024. Those curated custom content kits could be considered a precursor to the new marketplace, but on a much smaller scale. Making mods for The Sims 4 is a hobby for myshunosun, who works a full-time job in design. Fans of her work can subscribe to Patreon, where she has 200,000 members, more than 200 of which are paid. From that, she makes several hundred dollars a month.

Related:Split Fiction's final level concept was originally meant for the whole game

"Mods, in my opinion, have always been at the core of The Sims, and I believe that The Sims 4 specifically has always heavily relied on the expertise of custom content creators and modders," myshunosun said. "Since The Sims is a life simulation franchise, I think players naturally gravitate toward making the game 'their own,' and mods have always allowed more forms of expression, beyond what developers intended."

Like myshunosun, custom content creator SimMattically is supported by Patreon. He, like many other creators, offers exclusive early access to mods for paying members. Supporting creators isn't—or shouldn't—be "inherently controversial," SimMattically said. That support lets him take the "necessary time" to develop and refine his complex mods that often involve The Sims 4's user interface. SimMattically has more than 150,000 members on Patreon, more than 1,000 of which are paying members. The "paid early access" model for custom content and mods is somewhat unique to The Sims 4's community. "[It's] something you don’t see as much in other games," he said. "But I think The Sims is exceptional in this regard because the content is truly high-quality, often professional-grade 3D models. Creators deserve optional compensation, especially when it remains just that—optional."

Related:This is how RNG works as an 'equalizer' in Dispatch

The Sims 4 Marketplace risks changing the dynamic of the modding community, he said. Electronic Arts may be trying to fill a need of the community—a built-in way for mod galleries in the game itself—but this approach won't "do much good for the community in the long run," he said.

"The Marketplace changes the dynamic by moving away from optional community support and toward a centralized, virtual currency system," SimMattically told Game Developer. "This isn't motivated by a desire to embrace the community or help console players. It’s powered by corporate profit motives. The Sims 4 is already known for maximizing profit while minimizing the budget for quality control. This Marketplace would be a much better feature if EA’s approach wasn’t so offensive to the player and actually offered a choice between free and paid content."

SimMattically created a new mod in response to the marketplace announcement: a "billboard-style section" for his main menu override UI. This mod will rotate creations from different creators right on the main menu, through a community tab. Creators can submit their work to SimMattically to potentially be featured there. He originally created this mod as a way for players to customize the main menu as much as they can their own Sims. "I wanted to give players the same level of control over their interface that they have over their Sims' lives," he said. "I imagine The Sims to be the player's space, so the welcoming screen should also be ours."

It's a cheeky way to stick it to the marketplace, which SimMattically won't participate in. (UI mods aren't supported there, he said, but he wouldn't participate regardless, he said.)

The other piece that can't be ignored is the Electronic Arts take-private funded by an investor consortium composed of Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund, American private equity fund Silver Lake, and Jared Kushner's investment firm Affinity Partners. Saudi Arabia's involvement in the company is a nonstarter for lots of The Sims 4 community; several The Sims 4 influencers and creators already stepped away from partnerships with Electronic Arts over the buyout, due to Saudi Arabia's notoriously bad record with human rights. Myshunosun is one of those creators. They called the news "disappointing and appalling" in a post on their website.

The Sims 4 Marketplace comes at a time when the community is already distrustful of the company. Though The Sims 4 is free-to-play, it began as a traditional, paid experience—the new DLCs, which added content that was available in earlier games, required people to pay extra. If a person wanted to purchase all packs and kits, it would cost nearly $1,000, according to ScreenRant. A marketplace that, according to some creators, doesn't understand or value the community just adds to the community's frustrations.

"Creators don’t need EA, but EA needs creators to [get] the Marketplace off the ground," myshunosun said. "The Marketplace sets a worrying precedent for how a franchise like The Sims values creators' work and the experience of its player base."

About the Author

Columnist, Game Developer

Nicole Carpenter is a veteran reporter who has been covering the video game industry for over a decade. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/the-sims-4-modding-community-is-disappointed-by-new-microtransaction-marketplace
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Unions band together to launch new game industry conference 'by workers, for workers'
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 11, 2026

1 Min Read

Direct-join union United Videogame Workers of CWA (UVW-CWA) has launched the Game Workers Conference (GWC) and has pledged to create an event "by workers, for workers."

The event was announced at GDC Festival of Gaming and has been organized in partnership with other unions and worker-centered organizations. 

GWC will be held online online on Friday May 22, 2026 and Saturday May 23, 2026. Organizers will also host optional watch parties at physical venues in cities including Los Angeles, Montréal, and New York City. 

Notably, all conference sessions will be freely accessible. 

"The Game Workers Conference is an entirely new game industry event made by and for the countless workers who make this massively influential industry and artistic medium succeed," reads a statement on the GWC website. 

GWC organizers said speakers and sessions will be announced in the weeks leading up to the event, but noted that talks will cover a range of subjects including industry skills, project postmortems, movement work, union campaigns, and more. 

Related:Microsoft says Xbox Play Anywhere titles can double spending and engagement

Some attendees have been confirmed, including members of The Game Awards Future Class, who'll be discussing their experiences with the seemingly shuttered program. SAG-AFTRA organizers will also be sharing lessons from their history of organizing in the games industry, including their recent voice actor strike and fight against AI. 

In addition, video game journalists will be taking part in a panel to discuss the ties between press and industry and their relationship with the game worker movement.

Tickets for GWC are available right now. 

Game Developer and GDC Festival of Gaming are sibling companies under Informa Festivals.

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/unions-rally-together-to-launch-game-industry-conference
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Here's where to find Game Developer's coverage from GDC Festival of Gaming!
item.content:encoded:
We're somehow already half way through the event, but with the Festival Hall expo floor of GDC Festival of Gaming is officially in full swing! The Game Developer editorial team of Danielle Riendeau, Chris Kerr, Bryant Francis, and myself (plus a few wonderful freelancer writers!) are hard at work interviewing developers and covering sessions (and wearing out our favorite pairs of shoes in the process).

There's a lot going on at GDC this year, whether you're at the show or catching up on the latest juicy tidbits from home. For folks at the show, it can sometimes feel like you need to be in three places at once to hit everything on your agenda. We know. We triple-booked ourselves too. 

Read GDC Talk Highlights & Interviews from the Show

To alleviate some of that FOMO, we'll be camping out in as many talks as we can and publishing writeups on what we hear. Keep an eye on our GDC Festival of Gaming event page this week and beyond to read the latest out of the event. We're also chatting with developers, publishers, and other game industry figures throughout the week to hear perspectives on industry trends, talk the latest innovations, and generally share a love of video games. Some of those interviews you can already find here on Game Developer, and some you can watch live in the Festival Hall this week.

Related:Split Fiction's final level concept was originally meant for the whole game

Listen to the Game Developer Podcast Live

Game Developer Podcast host Bryant Francis will be running live podcast episodes every day from now until the show closes, and you can even listen in live! He and a few guests of honor will be camped out in GDC's new live podcasting station in the GDC Commons area right to the right of the escalators descending from South Hall. 

The booth is clear enclosure equipped with headphones so attendees can have a seat and listen in to live recordings from the Game Developer Podcast (and others!) throughout the week. Find Bryant and the Game Developer Podcast there Wednesday at 2:00 PM PT with Owlchemy Labs CEO Andrew Eiche, Thursday at 12:00 PM PT with game writer Alexa Ray Corriea, and Friday at 11:30 AM PT with the full GD editorial team for a GDC debrief.

Settle in for Live Game Developer Interviews

Just around the corner, you can sit down on a legitimately comfortable bench seat to watch live interviews on the GDC Commons Stage. I'll be there from now until Friday interviewing developers, publishers, and tools providers to share their expertise with GDC attendees, and we'll be bringing those recorded interviews to the website in the coming weeks. 

Related:This is how RNG works as an 'equalizer' in Dispatch

Find me there Wednesday at 4:00 to speak with Game Republic managing director Jamie Sefton and PitStop Productions CEO John Sanderson about UK-US game development trends, then again at 5:00 with UKIE CEO Nick Poole for a deeper at the future of UK-US development team-ups. We'll have interviews from this morning posted to the site in the coming weeks, including chats with Moon Studios CEO Thomas Mahler and Big Blue Sky Games publishing head Aaron Boulding, as well as a conversation with Irdeto on the future of game security.

On Thursday at 3:30 PM we'll be chatting with the folks from Helpshift on their new approach to support, engagement, community, and trust & safety. On Friday we'll close things out with two more interviews: a chat with the quadruple-threat writer, narrative designer, producer, and production director Amanda Farough at 12:30 PM, and an interview with Balor Games general manager of new games initiatives Alyssa Welles at 2:00 PM.

About the Author

Editorial Director, GameDeveloper.com

As the Editorial Director of Game Developer, Alissa McAloon brings a decade of experience in the video game industry and media. When not working in the world of B2B game journalism, Alissa enjoys spending her time in the worlds of immersive sandbox games or dabbling in the occasional TTRPG.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/design/here-s-where-to-find-game-developer-s-coverage-from-gdc-festival-of-gaming-
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Microsoft quietly retires 'This is an Xbox' marketing campaign
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 11, 2026

2 Min Read

Remember, 'This is an Xbox'? Microsoft made those four words the cornerstone of a marketing campaign it believed would hammer home one key point: Xbox is a platform, not just a console. 

Now, however, just weeks after the company appointed a new leader in former AI exec Asha Sharma and bid farewell to outgoing overseers Phil Spencer and Sarah Bond, it appears the company has quietly retied the campaign. 

When searching for the blog post that kicked off the marketing beat earlier today, I noticed it has been removed from Xbox Wire (Microsoft's Xbox-focused news repository). In fact, the only post currently available on the 'This is an Xbox' results page on Xbox Wire is the September 2025 update featuring news about the ROG Xbox Ally. 

The link for the original announcement remains, but when clicked you are instead greeted by a 404 error message and a yawning blank screen. "We can not find the page you are looking for," reads the small text at the top left corner of the page. Interesting. 

Related:UK advertising regulator issues Enforcement Notice targeting loot boxes

The post—which stated that 'This is an Xbox' would usher in an era "where people can play their favorite games, discover new adventures, and connect with friends, wherever and however they love to play"—remains accessible via Archive Today. 

Why is this notable? For starters, It has been claimed the campaign was pioneered by outgoing Xbox leader Sarah Bond and was even partly responsible for her departure. The Verge claims the strategy and the way it was communicated offended some Xbox employees, who purportedly weren't impressed by the vast pivot away from console. 

It also comes with Xbox firmly positioned as a multiplatform entity. Major franchises like Forza and Halo have been punted over to PlayStation 5, while others are available on Nintendo hardware. The company has even tried to open a dedicated Xbox marketplace on mobile, although we're still waiting for that pitch to bear fruit. That's despite being told it would be arriving in July 2024. 

Where is Xbox heading from here? Earlier today, Microsoft committed to placing console hardware at the heart of its business with its next-generation device, codenamed Project Helix. 

Yet, given the mysterious system will also play PC titles, it's also apparent that the company isn't ready to abandon its ambitions of a building larger playground for Xbox owners. 'This is an Xbox,' however, might have been uttered by the company for the last time. 

During a recent interview with Windows Central, Xbox boss Asha Sharma said "the plan's the plan until it's not the plan."  After a winding and decidedly vague statement like that, who knows what's next for the company.

Related:Tiktok refuses to axe racist AI-generated ads for Finji games

Game Developer has reached out to Xbox for comment. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/marketing/microsoft-quietly-retires-this-is-an-xbox-marketing-campaign
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
